title: change handling of ports when running containers
body:
the atomic team has reported two bugs when running containers.  In the case where
a user does:

* podman run -p 80 .... podman should assign a random port to the host and expose the container port 80 to it
* podman should detect if the user attempts to use the same host port more than once.

Signed-off-by: baude <bbaude@redhat.com>

comments.body:
This means I can't have two separate containers that portmap to port 80 on the host?  I am not crazy about this fix.  Why can't I have two containers and only one running at a time?

This is not going to fix the problem that I might have a real host listening on port 80.  I could see if this was looking for matches on randomly created ports, but not if the user asks to create a port binding.  

BTW Does docker block this?
Actually, that's a good point @rhatdan - if we do this in Conmon via https://github.com/kubernetes-incubator/cri-o/issues/1538 we can only block it when the containers are running
changes LGTM, tests look to be unhappy.
@rhatdan Yes docker blocks it which is why I got the issue filed against us.

@baude No, it doesn't - if I run `docker create -t -i -p 80:80 fedora bash` and `docker run -t -i --rm -p 80:80 fedora bash` in separate terminals, both succeed. The container that I created but not started will error if I actually start it because of a duplicate port, but if it's not running Docker is perfectly happy.

@rhatdan prefer it only check when start gets run on the container?  it might be harder, but compromises the two.

are we in agreement the assignment of the random host port when the user doesnt provide one is correct?
Yes I believe docker right now creates a random port if you don't specify the second part.
